intro: Slack and Discord land in similar territory on the hard numbers, so the decision comes down to fit with your workflow rather than a headline metric. Slack is built for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem and is not a fit for microsoft-standardized orgs or teams prioritizing suite-bundled collaboration. Discord is built for dev, gaming, and creator communities prioritizing real-time voice and chat engagement and is not a fit for teams wanting searchable, seo-indexed knowledge or formal customer support. On the scoring side, Slack rates 5/5 for developer experience and 5/5 for transparency; Discord rates 5/5 and 5/5 respectively. The honest trade-off: with Slack you accept that pricing feels punitive at scale, while Discord comes with the reality that seo invisible, content locked behind login. Neither side is free. Slack's headline strength in the data is that default chat for modern knowledge work. Discord's headline strength is that massive free scale with voice, video, chat.
quick_take: Slack is for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem; Discord is for dev, gaming, and creator communities prioritizing real-time voice and chat engagement; decide.
verdict_a: Choose Slack if you are tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem.
verdict_b: Choose Discord if you are dev, gaming, and creator communities prioritizing real-time voice and chat engagement.

faq.a: Both list a freemium pricing model in our data, and both publish a free tier. We don't have per-seat numbers in this dataset, so 'cheaper' depends on volume. Price both on your actual usage before deciding.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Slack to Discord?
faq.a: Slack has medium lock-in (data exportable, ecosystem lock); Discord has medium lock-in (data partially exportable via api). Migration effort scales with how much state and automation you've built in Slack; plan for data export, config rebuild, and a parallel-run period before cutting over.
Both Slack and Discord score 5/5 on developer experience in our data, so DX isn't the tiebreaker here. Decide on fit, pricing, or lock-in instead.

faq.q: Is Discord a good alternative to Slack?
faq.a: Discord is built for dev, gaming, and creator communities prioritizing real-time voice and chat engagement and explicitly not for teams wanting searchable, seo-indexed knowledge or formal customer support, so it's a fit only if your workflow matches its stated audience. If you were using Slack for tech-forward companies needing a strong chat platform with a rich app ecosystem, the switch is reasonable; outside that scope, look elsewhere.
